name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/5/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper comprises of three sections- Section A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Q. No. 1 to 3 are very short-answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Q. No. 4 to 11 are short-answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Answer the following questions (Do any two):

  1. Identify the order of reaction from the following unit for its rate constant: Lmol-1s-1
  2. The conversion of molecules A to B follow second order kinetics. concentration of A is increased to three times, how will it affect the rate of formation of B?
  3. Write the expression of integrated rate equation for zero order reaction.
 2
2.

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their property indicated:

  1. Ethanal, Propanone, Propanal, Butanone (reactivity towards nucleophilic addition)
  2. 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxy benzoic acid (Acid strength)
 2
3.

Explain the following reactions:

  1. Wolff Kishner reduction
  2. Cannizzaro reaction
 2

SECTION B
4.

Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions:

  1. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
  3. Write the relationship between k and t1/2 (half life period)
 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: K2[PdCl4]
  2. Using crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d5 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P.
  3. What are Homoleptic complexes?
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Why chelate complexes are more stable than complexes with unidentate ligands?
  2. What is spectrochemical series? What is the difference between a weak field ligand and a strong field ligand?
 3
6.(a)
  1. Define coagulation.
  2. State Hardy-Schulze rule.
  3. What is Electrophoresis?
 3

OR
(b)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

 3
7.
  1. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction.
  2. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason.
 3
8.

Give reasons for the following statements:

  1. Copper does not displace hydrogen from acids.
  2. Transition metals and most of their compounds show paramagnetic behaviour.
  3. Zn, Cd and Hg are soft metals.
 3
9.(a)

Account for the following:

  1. pkb of aniline is more than that of methylamine.
  2. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution:
    CH3 NH2, (CH3)3 N, (CH3)2 NH
  2. What is Hinsberg's reagent?
  3. What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
 3
10.

A compound A on reduction with iron scrap and hydrochloric acid gives compound B with molecular formula C6H7N. Compound B on reaction with CHCl3 and alcoholic KOH produces an obnoxious smell of carbylamine due to the formation of C. Identify A, B and C and write the chemical reactions involved.

 3
11.(a)

Complete the following:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex}
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following compound:
  3. Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid
 3

OR
(b)

Convert the following:

  1. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  2. Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid
  3. Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal
 3

SECTION C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If Ecell0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu2+. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically.

 5
(i)

Is silver plate the anode or cathode?

 1
(ii)

What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?

 1
(iii)

When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?

 1
(iv)
  1. What will happen to the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ when Ecell = 0.
  2. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
 2

OR
(iv)

The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm2mol-1. Calculate the conductivity of this solution.

 2

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/5/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper comprises of three sections- Section A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Q. No. 1 to 3 are very short-answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Q. No. 4 to 11 are short-answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Answer the following questions (Do any two):

  1. Identify the order of reaction from the following unit for its rate constant: Lmol-1s-1
  2. The conversion of molecules A to B follow second order kinetics. concentration of A is increased to three times, how will it affect the rate of formation of B?
  3. Write the expression of integrated rate equation for zero order reaction.
Solution:
  1. Second order
  2. Nine or 9 times
  3. k = {tex}\frac{[R]_0-[R]}{t}{/tex}
 2
2.

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their property indicated:

  1. Ethanal, Propanone, Propanal, Butanone (reactivity towards nucleophilic addition)
  2. 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxy benzoic acid (Acid strength)
Solution:
  1. The increasing order towards nucleophilic addition of the following compounds is Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanal (Due to +l effect).
  2. The increasing order of acidic strength of the following compounds is 4-methoxy benzoic acid < benzoic acid < 4-nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid. (Due to the presence of electron withdrawing group).
 2
3.

Explain the following reactions:

  1. Wolff Kishner reduction
  2. Cannizzaro reaction
Solution:
  1. Wolff Kishner reduction
    {tex}CH_3-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-CH_3\xrightarrow[{(ii)\;KOH\;/glycol}]{(i)\;NH_3\;-\;NH_2}\;CH_3CH_2CH_3{/tex}
  2. Cannizzaro reaction
    {tex}2H-\overset O{\overset{\vert \vert}C}-H\xrightarrow{50\%\ KOH}\ H-\overset O{\overset{\vert \vert}C}-OH + CH_3OH{/tex}
 2

SECTION : B
4.

Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions:

  1. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
  3. Write the relationship between k and t1/2 (half life period)
Solution:
  1. First order
  2. Slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex}
  3. k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: K2[PdCl4]
  2. Using crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d5 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P.
  3. What are Homoleptic complexes?
Solution:
  1. IUPAC name of K2[PdCl4] is Potassium tetrachloroethylene palladate (II).
  2. If {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P, the electronic configuration of d5 ion will be {tex}t_{2 g}^5 \ e_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation. Thus, no electron will enter into {tex}e_g{/tex} orbital.
  3. The complexes in which metal ion is bound to only one type of ligands are called homoleptic complexes.
    e.g. In [Co(NH3)6]3+, only one type of ligand, NH3 surrounds the Co3+ ion.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Why chelate complexes are more stable than complexes with unidentate ligands?
  2. What is spectrochemical series? What is the difference between a weak field ligand and a strong field ligand?
Solution:
  1. Chelate complexes are more stable than unidentate ligands because they form a ring with the central metal ion and are held together by a strong force of attraction, making them less likely to dissolve. Unidentate ligands, on the other hand, are connected to the core metal at a single location, resulting in less force of attraction and a greater likelihood of dissociation.
  2. A spectrochemical series is the arrangement of common ligands in the increasing order of their crystal-field splitting energy (CFSE) values. The ligands present on the R.H.S of the series are strong field ligands while that on the L.H.S are weak field ligands. Also, strong field ligands cause higher splitting in the d orbitals than weak field ligands.
    I - < Br- < S2- < SCN- < Cl- < N3 < F- < OH- < C2O42- {tex}\sim{/tex} H2O < NCS- {tex}\sim{/tex} H- < CN- < NH3 < en {tex}\sim{/tex} SO32- < NO2- < phen < CO
 3
6.(a)
  1. Define coagulation.
  2. State Hardy-Schulze rule.
  3. What is Electrophoresis?
Solution:

Chapter Deleted from the Textbook.

 3

OR
(b)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3
7.
  1. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction.
  2. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason.
Solution:
  1. The two consequences of lanthanide contraction are:
    1. Due to the close similarity in electronic configuration and ionic radii, the lanthanides have identical chemical properties which make their separation difficult.
    2. Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of Ln3+ ions decrease regularly with an increase in atomic number. According to Fajan's rule, a decrease in the size of Ln3+ ions increase the covalent character and decrease the basic character between Ln3+ and OH- ion in Ln(OH)3. Since the order of size of Ln3+ ions are La3+ > Ce3+ ... > Lu3+.
  2. The element which shows the largest number of oxidation states in the 3d series is Manganese (Mn). It shows the following oxidation states +2, +3, +4, +5, +6 and +7. It is because it contains the maximum no. of unpaired e-s in the outermost shell, i.e., 3d94s2.
 3
8.

Give reasons for the following statements:

  1. Copper does not displace hydrogen from acids.
  2. Transition metals and most of their compounds show paramagnetic behaviour.
  3. Zn, Cd and Hg are soft metals.
Solution:
  1. Copper cannot displace hydrogen from acid because copper is a less reactive element than hydrogen and it is present below hydrogen in the activity series of metals.
  2. Transition metals and many other metals exhibit paramagnetic behaviour due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the d-orbital.
  3. Metal atoms like Zn, Cd, Hg have completely filled d-orbitals (d10 configuration). Thus, d-electrons are not available for metallic bond formation. As a result, these metals are quite soft and also have low melting points.
 3
9.(a)

Account for the following:

  1. pkb of aniline is more than that of methylamine.
  2. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
Solution:
  1. lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. 
  2. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt. 
  3. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution:
    CH3 NH2, (CH3)3 N, (CH3)2 NH
  2. What is Hinsberg's reagent?
  3. What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
Solution:
  1. The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH3)3N < CH3NH2 < (CH3)2NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups).
  2. Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C6H6SO2Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides.
  3. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction.
 3
10.

A compound A on reduction with iron scrap and hydrochloric acid gives compound B with molecular formula C6H7N. Compound B on reaction with CHCl3 and alcoholic KOH produces an obnoxious smell of carbylamine due to the formation of C. Identify A, B and C and write the chemical reactions involved.

Solution:

 
The compound A is Nitrobenzene, B is Aniline and C is Phenyl isocyanide.

 3
11.(a)

Complete the following:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex}
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following compound:
  3. Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid
Solution:
  1.  
  2. 3-Bromobenzaldehyde / 3-Bromobenzene carbaldehyde
  3. Benzoic acid gives brisk effervescence liberating carbon dioxide on reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate while phenol does not. / In terms of chemical reaction mentioning brisk effervescence.
 3

OR
(b)

Convert the following:

  1. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  2. Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid
  3. Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal
Solution:
  2.  
  3. {tex}2\underset{Acetaldehyde}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CHO}}\xrightarrow{dil.\;\mathrm{NaOH}}\underset{\underset{Aldol\;condensation\;reaction}{Aldol\;(intermediate)}}{\left[{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{\mathrm{CH}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}-\right]}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]\Delta{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{But-2-enal}{\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex}
 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If Ecell0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu2+. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically.

 5
(i)

Is silver plate the anode or cathode?

Solution:

Silver plate acts as cathode.

 1
(ii)

What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?

Solution:

Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero.

 1
(iii)

When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?

Solution:

An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction.
Eext > Ecell

 1
(iv)
  1. What will happen to the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ when Ecell = 0.
  2. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
Solution:
  1. When Ecell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ remains same.
  2. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution.
 2

OR
(iv)

The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm2mol-1. Calculate the conductivity of this solution.

Solution:

Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}
Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.208 Scm-1

 2

Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions: What is the order of the reaction? What is the slope of the curve? Write the relationship between k and t 1/2 (half life period) Solution: First order Slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 3 5. (a) Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: K 2 [PdCl 4 ] Using crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d 5 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P. What are Homoleptic complexes? Solution: IUPAC name of K 2 [PdCl 4 ] is Potassium tetrachloroethylene palladate (II). If {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P, the electronic configuration of d 5 ion will be {tex}t_{2 g}^5 \ e_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation. Thus, no electron will enter into {tex}e_g{/tex} orbital. The complexes in which metal ion is bound to only one type of ligands are called homoleptic complexes. e.g. In [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ , only one type of ligand, NH 3 surrounds the Co 3+ ion. 3 OR (b) Why chelate complexes are more stable than complexes with unidentate ligands? What is spectrochemical series ? What is the difference between a weak field ligand and a strong field ligand? Solution: Chelate complexes are more stable than unidentate ligands because they form a ring with the central metal ion and are held together by a strong force of attraction, making them less likely to dissolve. Unidentate ligands, on the other hand, are connected to the core metal at a single location, resulting in less force of attraction and a greater likelihood of dissociation. A spectrochemical series is the arrangement of common ligands in the increasing order of their crystal-field splitting energy (CFSE) values. The ligands present on the R.H.S of the series are strong field ligands while that on the L.H.S are weak field ligands. Also, strong field ligands cause higher splitting in the d orbitals than weak field ligands. (a) Define coagulation. State Hardy-Schulze rule. What is Electrophoresis? Solution: Chapter Deleted from the Textbook. 3 OR (b) Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption. Solution: Chapter Delete from the Textbook. 3 7. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason. Solution: The two consequences of lanthanide contraction are: Due to the close similarity in electronic configuration and ionic radii, the lanthanides have identical chemical properties which make their separation difficult. Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of Ln 3+ ions decrease regularly with an increase in atomic number. According to Fajan's rule, a decrease in the size of Ln 3+ ions increase the covalent character and decrease the basic character between Ln 3+ and OH - ion in Ln(OH) 3 . Since the order of size of Ln 3+ ions are La 3+ > Ce 3+ ... > Lu 3+ . The element which shows the largest number of oxidation states in the 3d series is Manganese (Mn). It shows the following oxidation states +2, +3, +4, +5, +6 and +7. It is because it contains the maximum no. of unpaired e - s in the outermost shell, i.e., 3d 9 4s 2 . 3 8. Give reasons for the following statements: Copper does not displace hydrogen from acids. Transition metals and most of their compounds show paramagnetic behaviour. Zn, Cd and Hg are soft metals. Solution: Copper cannot displace hydrogen from acid because copper is a less reactive element than hydrogen and it is present below hydrogen in the activity series of metals. Transition metals and many other metals exhibit paramagnetic behaviour due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the d-orbital. Metal atoms like Zn, Cd, Hg have completely filled d-orbitals (d 10 configuration). Thus, d-electrons are not available for metallic bond formation. As a result, these metals are quite soft and also have low melting points. 3 9. (a) Account for the following: pk b of aniline is more than that of methylamine. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines. Solution: lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines. 3 OR (b) Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 3 N, (CH 3 ) 2 NH What is Hinsberg's reagent? What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines? Solution: The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH 3 ) 3 N < CH 3 NH 2 < (CH 3 ) 2 NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups). Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 6 SO 2 Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction. 3 10. A compound A on reduction with iron scrap and hydrochloric acid gives compound B with molecular formula C 6 H 7 N. Compound B on reaction with CHCl 3 and alcoholic KOH produces an obnoxious smell of carbylamine due to the formation of C . Identify A , B and C and write the chemical reactions involved. Solution: The compound A is Nitrobenzene, B is Aniline and C is Phenyl isocyanide. 3 11. (a) Complete the following: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex} Write IUPAC name of the following compound: Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid Solution: 3-Bromobenzaldehyde / 3-Bromobenzene carbaldehyde Benzoic acid gives brisk effervescence liberating carbon dioxide on reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate while phenol does not. / In terms of chemical reaction mentioning brisk effervescence. 3 OR (b) Convert the following: Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal Solution: {tex}2\underset{Acetaldehyde}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CHO}}\xrightarrow{dil.\;\mathrm{NaOH}}\underset{\underset{Aldol\;condensation\;reaction}{Aldol\;(intermediate)}}{\left[{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{\mathrm{CH}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}-\right]}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]\Delta{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{But-2-enal}{\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} 3 SECTION : C 12. Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If E cell 0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu 2+ . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. 5 (i) Is silver plate the anode or cathode? Solution: Silver plate acts as cathode. 1 (ii) What will happen if the salt bridge is removed? Solution: Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero. 1 (iii) When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell? Solution: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction. E ext > E cell 1 (iv) What will happen to the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + when E cell = 0. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution? Solution: When E cell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + remains same. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution. 2 OR (iv) The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm 2 mol -1 . Calculate the conductivity of this solution. Solution: Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex} = 0.208 Scm -1 2
